alliterative.s: (I like to call you Daddy dear; it's so alliterative.)

answer.n: I've been writing this letter off and on for three days, and I fear by now vous etes bien bored!

answer.n: So you see--I am not so different from other girls--I get letters, too.

apart.s: The rule is that it must be at the most six feet apart, but they were the longest six feet I ever saw.

deliver.n: Yesterday he brought me some shoe-strings and a jar of cold cream (I sunburned all the skin off my nose before I got my new hat) and a blue Windsor tie and a bottle of blacking all for ten cents.
deliver.s: Our postman not only delivers letters, but he runs errands for us in town, at five cents an errand.

delivery.n: Our postman not only delivers letters, but he runs errands for us in town, at five cents an errand.
delivery.s: Rural delivery is a blessing to the farmers!

denmark.n: We have the kingdom working beautifully.
denmark.s: The King and Queen are both dead--an accident at sea; no funeral necessary--so Hamlet and I are ruling in Denmark without any bother.
I remain, sir, Yours most graciously, OPHELIA, Queen of Denmark.

denomination.n: Why on earth don't they go to men's colleges and urge the students not to allow their manly natures to be crushed out by too much mental application?
denomination.s: It doesn't matter what part of the United States or Canada they come from, or what denomination they are, we always get the same sermon.

denouement.n: It's really awfully queer not to know what one is--sort of exciting and romantic.
denouement.s: If we were in a novel, that would be the denouement, wouldn't it?

deny.n: I offered Being for it; The mighty merchant smiled.
deny.s: I asked no other thing, No other was denied.

departure.n: Jerusha caught only a fleeting impression of the man--and the impression consisted entirely of tallness.
departure.s: The long lower hall had not been lighted, and as she came downstairs, a last Trustee stood, on the point of departure, in the open door that led to the porte-cochere.

dependent.n: The Professor shuffled up his subjects and dealt them out promiscuously, and that fell to me.
dependent.s: a paper on the Care of Dependent Children.

deportment.n: Since I probably have no place to go this summer, she will let me come back to the asylum and work for my board until college opens.
deportment.s: She hopes that I am doing well in deportment and studies.

deprive.n: You can apply the money that you would have spent for me towards educating some other little girl from the John Grier Home.
deprive.s: Since you are so afraid that by taking this scholarship I am depriving someone else of an education, I know a way out.

depth.n: The things that most girls with a properly assorted family and a home and friends and a library know by absorption, I have never heard of.
depth.s: You wouldn't believe, Daddy, what an abyss of ignorance my mind is; I am just realizing the depths myself.

derive.n: She advanced to the office quite cheered by the tiny episode, and presented a smiling face to Mrs. Lippett.
derive.s: If one could derive any sort of entertainment out of the oppressive fact of a Trustee, it was something unexpected to the good.

des.s: Je ne peux pas send des mes nouvelles parceque je suis dans French class et j'ai peur que Monsieur le Professeur is going to call on me tout de suite.

descend.n: But if you MUST have news, here it is:
descend.s: Judy is becoming so philosophical of late, that she wishes to discourse largely of the world in general, instead of descending to the trivial details of daily life.

describe.n: He's extremely nice with the farmers around here; he meets them in a sort of man-to-man fashion that disarms them immediately.
describe.s: He is just as simple and unaffected and sweet as he can be--that seems a funny way to describe a man, but it's true.

description.n: Classes don't begin until Monday morning, and this is Saturday night.
description.s: I will write you a description later when I'm feeling less muddled; also I will tell you about my lessons.

deserve.n: He does not, I may tell you, care for girls.'
deserve.s: Heretofore his philanthropies have been directed solely towards the boys; I have never been able to interest him in the slightest degree in any of the girls in the institution, no matter how deserving.

deserve.n: But I like them just the same.
deserve.s: You have already given me such lots of things--everything I have, you know--that I don't quite feel that I deserve extras.

deserve.n: I must be a very, very, VERY good person to pay.
deserve.s: You and the Good Lord give me more than I deserve.

deserve.n: Of course I'll do exactly as you say, but please, PLEASE let me go, Daddy.
deserve.s: It's the kind of nice, jolly, care-free time that I've never had; and I think every girl deserves it once in her life.

deserve.n: Goodbye, and a very merry Christmas.
deserve.s: You deserve to be happy for all the good things you do.
When I've worked all the summer I deserve two weeks.

deserve.n: I wouldn't ask it except for the girl; I don't care much what happens to the mother--she is such a jelly-fish.
deserve.s: That girl deserves help a lot more than I ever did.
You deserve ten thousand years out of purgatory.

desirable.n: I don't believe there's any real, underneath difference, do you?
desirable.s: If I can turn my back on that and shut out the remembrance, I think, I might be just as desirable as any other girl.

desirable.n: It relieved my mind to find he was rich, for the tea and things cost sixty cents apiece.
desirable.s: She was furious with me for taking him off; it seems he's an unusually rich and desirable uncle.

desirable.n: There are advantages, you see, about rooming with Julia.
desirable.s: Julia's desirable uncle called again this afternoon--and brought a five-pound box of chocolates.

desirable.n: Just now he turns up his nose at overalls, but he'll come to them.
desirable.s: But to be the manager of a flourishing overall factory is a very desirable position, don't you think?

desire.n: He will never answer your letters, nor in the slightest particular take any notice of them.
desire.s: Since you have no family with whom to correspond, he desires you to write in this way; also, he wishes to keep track of your progress.

desk.n: We had tombstone for dessert (milk and gelatin flavoured with vanilla).
desk.s: Friday is sweeping day, and the maid had mixed all the papers on my desk.

despair.n: It makes me almost hope I'm not a genius; they must be very wearing to have about--and awfully destructive to the furniture.
despair.s: Listen to this: 'Last night I was seized by a fit of despair that found utterance in moans, and that finally drove me to throw the dining-room clock into the sea.'

desperate.n: A mother and father and four visible children--the two older boys have disappeared into the world to make their fortune and have not sent any of it back.
desperate.s: There is a family here who are in awfully desperate straits.

desperation.s: If something doesn't happen soon, I shall throw myself into the silo pit out of sheer desperation.

dessert.n: Once we walked into town--four miles--and stopped at a restaurant where the college girls go for dinner.
dessert.s: Broiled lobster (35 cents), and for dessert, buckwheat cakes and maple syrup (15 cents).

dessert.n: We were kept in chapel twenty minutes later than usual to listen to a speech about womanly women.
dessert.s: We had tombstone for dessert (milk and gelatin flavoured with vanilla).

dessert.n: We discussed it heatedly for half an hour and it's still unsettled.
dessert.s: We were having lemon jelly for dessert when the question came up.

dessert.n: Only vegetable dyes are used in colouring the food.
dessert.s: We had delicious pink ice-cream for dessert last night.

destructive.n: Listen to this: 'Last night I was seized by a fit of despair that found utterance in moans, and that finally drove me to throw the dining-room clock into the sea.'
destructive.s: It makes me almost hope I'm not a genius; they must be very wearing to have about--and awfully destructive to the furniture.

detail.n: You are to remain here through the summer, and Miss Pritchard has kindly offered to superintend your outfit.
detail.s: But he planned the matter in detail, and I did not feel free to make any suggestions.

detail.n: So much easier and more accurate than trying to keep it in your head.
detail.s: Then after I've graduated I shall have my whole education in a row in the bookcase, and when I need to use any detail, I can turn to it without the slightest hesitation.

detail.n: 'Be careful not to keep your eyes glued to detail,' says History Professor.
detail.s: 'The test of true scholarship,' says Chemistry Professor, 'is a painstaking passion for detail.'

detail.n: 'Stand far enough away to get a perspective of the whole.'
detail.s: 'Be careful not to keep your eyes glued to detail,' says History Professor.

detail.n: It gives a feeling of security and restfulness to the history recitation, that is entirely lacking in chemistry.
detail.s: If I say that William the Conqueror came over in 1492, and Columbus discovered America in 1100 or 1066 or whenever it was, that's a mere detail that the Professor overlooks.

detail.n: But anyway, they are going to be happy.
detail.s: I plan it out to the littlest detail--the meals and clothes and study and amusements and punishments; for even my superior orphans are sometimes bad.

detail.n: You're living up to your side of the bargain--I'm being educated--and I suppose you're thinking I'm not living up to mine!
detail.s: I know that I was to write nice, long, detailed letters without ever expecting any answer.

detail.n: But if you MUST have news, here it is:
detail.s: Judy is becoming so philosophical of late, that she wishes to discourse largely of the world in general, instead of descending to the trivial details of daily life.

detail.n: I wish you were here, too; we'd all have such a jolly time together.
detail.s: Never say, Daddy, that I don't give details.

detail.n: One can't help thinking, Daddy, what a colourless life a man is forced to lead, when one reflects that chiffon and Venetian point and hand embroidery and Irish crochet are to him mere empty words.
detail.s: You must be deeply impressed by these millinery details.

detail.n: But it was entirely regular; we had leave-of-absence from college, and Mrs. McBride chaperoned us.
detail.s: We had a charming time--but I shall have to omit details; they are too many and complicated.

detail.n: The girl that left Lock Willow at dawn was a very different person from the one who came back at night.
detail.s: If I live to be ninety-nine I shall never forget the tiniest detail.

detain.n: 'I trust that you are properly grateful for this very rare good fortune that has befallen you?
detain.s: Mrs. Lippett detained her with a gesture; it was an oratorical opportunity not to be slighted.

detective.n: I saw a street car conductor today with one brown eye and one blue.
detective.s: Wouldn't he make a nice villain for a detective story?

detective.n: A hundred little things might have told me, if I had had any wits.
detective.s: I wouldn't make a very good detective, would I, Daddy?

determine.n: 'We have to leave here at a quarter past ten in order to get to church by eleven.'
determine.s: This morning, said Mrs. Semple to Mr. Pendleton, with a very determined accent:

determine.n: Good night, Daddy dear, and don't be annoyed because your chick is wanting to scratch for herself.
determine.s: She's growing up into an awfully energetic little hen--with a very determined cluck and lots of beautiful feathers (all due to you).

determine.n: I will look into the subject over Sunday, and declare my principles in my next.
determine.s: I haven't determined yet just which kind I am going to be.

detest.n: If any point should ever arise where an answer would seem to be imperative--such as in the event of your being expelled, which I trust will not occur--you may correspond with Mr. Griggs, his secretary.
detest.s: He detests letter-writing and does not wish you to become a burden.

detestable.n: They ought to do everything from love.
detestable.s: I don't think children ought to know the meaning of the word; it's odious, detestable.

develop.n: I am going to pretend that all life is just a game which I must play as skilfully and fairly as I can.
develop.s: It's the kind of character that I am going to develop.

develop.n: The happy people are the ones who are bubbling over with kindliness.
develop.s: I don't agree with the theory that adversity and sorrow and disappointment develop moral strength.

develop.n: Do you know about that one scandalous blot in my career the time I ran away from the asylum because they punished me for stealing cookies?
develop.s: I have a very WANDERING spirit, though I haven't as yet had much chance to develop it.

develop.n: Maybe it isn't polite to criticize people you've been visiting?
develop.s: Not for all the money in the world would I ever let any children of mine develop into Pendletons.

develop.n: It doesn't matter what part of the United States or Canada they come from, or what denomination they are, we always get the same sermon.
develop.s: We must take care, he says, not to develop our intellects at the expense of our emotional natures--but methought it was a poor, dry sermon (Pepys again).

develope.n: I hope that I don't hurt your feelings when I criticize the home of my youth?
develope.s: The unusual artistic ability which I exhibit was developed at an early age through drawing chalk pictures of Mrs. Lippett on the woodshed door.

develope.n: I've decided to sit down by the way and pile up a lot of little happinesses, even if I never become a Great Author.
develope.s: Did you ever know such a philosopheress as I am developing into?

develope.n: If I have five children, like Rousseau, I shan't leave them on the steps of a foundling asylum in order to insure their being brought up simply.
develope.s: Give my kindest regards to Mrs. Lippett (that, I think, is truthful; love would be a little strong) and don't forget to tell her what a beautiful nature I've developed.

devil.n: I've been here for an hour; it's a very comfortable crotch, especially after being upholstered with two sofa cushions.
devil.s: There's a frog croaking underneath, a locust singing overhead and two little 'devil downheads' darting up and down the trunk.

devise.n: The gymnasium is equipped with a very beautiful swimming tank of cement and marble, the gift of a former graduate.
devise.s: A proctor system has been devised, and failure to comply with the rules causes a great deal of inconvenience.

devise.n: What do you, a reformer, think of that?
devise.s: A friend of Pepys devised a very cunning manner whereby the king might pay his debts out of the sale to poor people of old decayed provisions.

devoid.n: To bring a man into line, there are just two methods: one must either coax or be disagreeable.
devoid.s: You belong, Mr. Smith, to a sex devoid of a sense of logic.

devote.n: Sallie and Julia and I went shopping together Saturday morning.
devote.s: It makes you want to devote your life to wearing clothes.

dictate.n: No one can dictate to me but you, Daddy--and you can't always!
dictate.s: I MUST show him that he can't dictate to me.
No one can dictate to me but you, Daddy--and you can't always!

dictatorial.n: I can be enticed step by step, but I WON'T be forced.
dictatorial.s: I almost weakened; if he hadn't been so dictatorial, maybe I should have entirely weakened.

dictionary.n: He wanted to send chocolates, but I insisted on synonyms.
dictionary.s: My sister Isabel gave me the silk stockings, and Aunt Susan the Matthew Arnold poems; Uncle Harry (little Harry is named after him) gave me the dictionary.

die.n: Thursday evening at twilight, sitting on the doorstep.
die.s: 'I shall see before I die the palms and temples of the South.'

die.n: Don't you think it would be interesting if you really could read the story of your life--written perfectly truthfully by an omniscient author?
die.s: I almost found out whom I'm going to marry and when I'm going to die.

die.n: Seems a little early to commence entertaining, doesn't it?
die.s: And this: 'Dined with my lady who is in handsome mourning for her brother who died yesterday of spotted fever.'

difference.n: If I can turn my back on that and shut out the remembrance, I think, I might be just as desirable as any other girl.
difference.s: I don't want you to think I am a coward, but I do want to be like the other girls, and that Dreadful Home looming over my childhood is the one great big difference.
I don't believe there's any real, underneath difference, do you?

different.n: I never dreamed there was such a place in the world.
different.s: You can't imagine how different it is from the John Grier Home.

different.n: I could FEEL 'John Grier Home' written on my face.
different.s: I was queer and different and everybody knew it.

different.n: Before I thought, I started to tell the others what an experience I was having.
different.s: Especially for me, because it was so awfully different from the asylum--I feel like an escaped convict every time I leave the campus.

different.n: They can take things naturally from people.
different.s: It's different with me than with other girls.

different.n: So you see I'm trying to be an author.
different.s: I've been writing and writing this summer; four short stories finished and sent to four different magazines.

different.n: We went for a long tramp this morning and got caught in a storm.
different.s: It's astonishing how many different things he knows.

different.n: I wanted to ask Mr. Pendleton if he knew you in New York--I should think he might; you must move in about the same exalted social circles, and you are both interested in reforms and things--but I couldn't, for I don't know your real name.
different.s: I like my different friends to know each other.

different.n: If I ever marry and have a family, I'm going to make them as exactly like the McBrides as I can.
different.s: She did seem a different kind of mother from Mrs. McBride!

different.n: Probably I am one by rights; I belong to the proletariat.
different.s: They're quite different from Anarchists; they don't believe in blowing people up.

different.n: Did I tell you that I have been elected a member of the Senior Dramatic Club?
different.s: So you see--I am not so different from other girls--I get letters, too.

different.n: Mrs. Semple called me at half-past four.
different.s: The girl that left Lock Willow at dawn was a very different person from the one who came back at night.

difficult.n: Given a tall rich man who hates girls, but is very generous to one quite impertinent girl, what does he look like?
difficult.s: It is very difficult thinking about you in the abstract like a theorem in geometry.

difficult.n: He said he was bashful about dancing with girls he didn't know!
difficult.s: I found him a very difficult guest; he was sulky because he had only three dances with me.

difficult.n: We use a quiet corner of the cliffs for a schoolroom--Mrs. Paterson wishes me to keep them out of doors--and I will say that I find it difficult to concentrate with the blue sea before me and ships a-sailing by!
difficult.s: She is a most uncommonly spoiled child; I shall have to teach her first how to study--she has never in her life concentrated on anything more difficult than ice-cream soda water.

dignity.n: Jerusha gazed out across a broad stretch of frozen lawn, beyond the tall iron paling that marked the confines of the asylum, down undulating ridges sprinkled with country estates, to the spires of the village rising from the midst of bare trees.
dignity.s: Mrs. Lippett, behind the scenes, did not always maintain that calm and pompous dignity with which she faced an audience of Trustees and lady visitors.
Ought I to treat you with more dignity and aloofness?--Yes, I'm sure I ought.

dimple.s: I looked in the glass this morning and found a perfectly new dimple that I'd never seen before.

dine.n: We had ham and eggs and biscuits and honey and jelly-cake and pie and pickles and cheese and tea for supper--and a great deal of conversation.
dine.s: The hired people eat in the kitchen, and the Semples and Judy in the dining-room.

dine.n: It makes me almost hope I'm not a genius; they must be very wearing to have about--and awfully destructive to the furniture.
dine.s: Listen to this: 'Last night I was seized by a fit of despair that found utterance in moans, and that finally drove me to throw the dining-room clock into the sea.'

dine.n: I ate my fish with the wrong fork, but the waiter very kindly gave me another so that nobody noticed.
dine.s: Picture that, then picture the dining-room of the John Grier Home with its oilcloth-covered tables, and white crockery that you CAN'T break, and wooden-handled knives and forks; and fancy the way I felt!

dine.n: But he ordered dinner at seven--he orders meals whenever he chooses; you would think the place were a restaurant--and that kept Carrie and Amasai from going driving.
dine.s: It discommoded the household dreadfully, because Lock Willow of a Sunday dines at two.

dined.n: Seems a little early to commence entertaining, doesn't it?
dined.s: And this: 'Dined with my lady who is in handsome mourning for her brother who died yesterday of spotted fever.'

dingy.s: Also his neckties are quite dingy--black and brown, where they used to be scarlet and purple.

dining.n: There is so much noise that you can't hear the girls across the table speak unless they make their hands into a megaphone and shout.
dining.s: Dining in a room with four hundred girls is an ordeal when you are tired.

dinner.n: Broiled lobster (35 cents), and for dessert, buckwheat cakes and maple syrup (15 cents).
dinner.s: Once we walked into town--four miles--and stopped at a restaurant where the college girls go for dinner.

dinner.n: In two minutes more when this letter is finished I am going to settle down to a book which I found in the attic.
dinner.s: Amasai spent all the morning washing the buggy; and Carrie stayed home from church ostensibly to cook the dinner, but really to iron the muslin dress.

dinner.n: Then we all cut evening chapel, the state of our boots being enough of an excuse.
dinner.s: We didn't get back to college till half-past six--half an hour late for dinner--and we went straight in without dressing, and with perfectly unimpaired appetites!

dinner.n: I never heard of meals being served in trains before, and I inadvertently said so.
dinner.s: We came back Saturday night and had our dinner in the train, at little tables with pink lamps and negro waiters.

dinner.n: We had fried soft-shell crabs, and chocolate ice-cream moulded in the shape of basket balls.
dinner.s: The athletic association gave a dinner in the gymnasium to all of the winners.

dinner.n: This morning, said Mrs. Semple to Mr. Pendleton, with a very determined accent:
dinner.s: It's Sunday night now, about eleven o'clock, and I am supposed to be getting some beauty sleep, but I had black coffee for dinner, so--no beauty sleep for me!

dinner.n: But he said it was all the better because it wasn't proper for them to go driving without a chaperon; and anyway, he wanted the horses himself to take me driving.
dinner.s: But he ordered dinner at seven--he orders meals whenever he chooses; you would think the place were a restaurant--and that kept Carrie and Amasai from going driving.

dinner.n: I am getting a little sleepy, though.
dinner.s: We got home at four and went driving at five and had dinner at seven, and at ten I was sent to bed and here I am, writing to you.

dinner.n: The hotel was so full that they slept in rows on the billiard tables, they say.
dinner.s: However--our guests came Friday afternoon in time for tea in the senior corridor, and then dashed down to the hotel for dinner.

dinner.n: Well, Daddy, it did appeal to me!
dinner.s: Also, that he would be in Paris at the same time, and that we would run away from the chaperon occasionally and have dinner together at nice, funny, foreign restaurants.

dinner.n: I was awfully glad to see him; he brought a momentary reminder that the world at large exists.
dinner.s: Fried chicken and ice-cream for dinner, both of which he appeared to appreciate.

diploma.n: But look at it just a second from my point of view.
diploma.s: I suppose you feel that when you set out to educate me, you'd like to finish the work, and put a neat period, in the shape of a diploma, at the end.

diploma.n: Commencement was as usual, with a few showers at vital moments.
diploma.s: My diploma is in the bottom bureau drawer with my two best dresses.

direct.n: He does not, I may tell you, care for girls.'
direct.s: Heretofore his philanthropies have been directed solely towards the boys; I have never been able to interest him in the slightest degree in any of the girls in the institution, no matter how deserving.

direct.n: He's sorry, but he finds that he can't get up there this autumn; he has accepted an invitation to go yachting with some friends.
direct.s: A note from Master Jervie directed to Lock Willow and forwarded here.

direction.n: And one day--well, I won't bore you with particulars--but somehow, one of the fattest, biggest, JUCIEST toads got into one of those big leather arm chairs in the Trustees' room, and that afternoon at the Trustees' meeting--But I dare say you were there and recall the rest?
direction.s: We were severely punished for our activities in this direction, but in spite of all discouragement the toads would collect.

directly.n: This will enable you to enter on the same standing as the other students.
directly.s: Your board and tuition will be paid directly to the college, and you will receive in addition during the four years you are there, an allowance of thirty-five dollars a month.

disagreeable.n: I scorn to coax men for what I wish.
disagreeable.s: To bring a man into line, there are just two methods: one must either coax or be disagreeable.

disagreement.n: On looking it up, I find that she was right.
disagreement.s: In trigonometry the Professor and I had a disagreement touching a little matter of logarithms.

disappear.n: The father worked in a glass factory and got consumption--it's awfully unhealthy work--and now has been sent away to a hospital.
disappear.s: A mother and father and four visible children--the two older boys have disappeared into the world to make their fortune and have not sent any of it back.

disappear.n: There are lots of troubles in the world!
disappear.s: Sentimental Tommy (the tortoise-shell cat) has disappeared; we are afraid he has been caught in a trap.

disappear.n: I think he's pretty unhappy, and I know I am!
disappear.s: I was feeling hurt because he had just disappeared into blankness without a word.

disappoint.n: I've read seventeen novels and bushels of poetry--really necessary novels like Vanity Fair and Richard Feverel and Alice in Wonderland.
disappoint.s: I'm sorry if you're disappointed, but otherwise I don't care a bit because I've learned such a lot of things not mentioned in the catalogue.

disappoint.n: In the spring when everything is so beautiful and green and budding, I feel like turning my back on lessons, and running away to play with the weather.
disappoint.s: Will you be awfully disappointed, Daddy, if I don't turn out to be a great author?

disappoint.n: Of course I know that you're my guardian, and that I have to regard your wishes in all matters, but I couldn't see any REASON.
disappoint.s: You can't imagine how disappointed I was at having to give up the McBrides' camp.

disappoint.n: I don't think he cares much for his relatives--and I am sure they don't care much for him!
disappoint.s: It was really disappointing after our nice time last summer.

disappointment.n: The happy people are the ones who are bubbling over with kindliness.
disappointment.s: I don't agree with the theory that adversity and sorrow and disappointment develop moral strength.

disarm.s: He's extremely nice with the farmers around here; he meets them in a sort of man-to-man fashion that disarms them immediately.

discommoded.n: But he ordered dinner at seven--he orders meals whenever he chooses; you would think the place were a restaurant--and that kept Carrie and Amasai from going driving.
discommoded.s: It discommoded the household dreadfully, because Lock Willow of a Sunday dines at two.

discourage.n: I wish you might have heard it.
discourage.s: It was about the necessity of overlooking mistakes in others, and not discouraging people by harsh judgments.

discourage.n: It isn't the big troubles in life that require character.
discourage.s: Did you ever hear of such a discouraging series of events?
(I'll never attempt that again; I was discouraged last year.)

discouragement.n: And one day--well, I won't bore you with particulars--but somehow, one of the fattest, biggest, JUCIEST toads got into one of those big leather arm chairs in the Trustees' room, and that afternoon at the Trustees' meeting--But I dare say you were there and recall the rest?
discouragement.s: We were severely punished for our activities in this direction, but in spite of all discouragement the toads would collect.

discourse.n: But if you MUST have news, here it is:
discourse.s: Judy is becoming so philosophical of late, that she wishes to discourse largely of the world in general, instead of descending to the trivial details of daily life.

discover.n: Knowing a member of the Pendleton family is the best introduction one can have at Lock Willow.
discover.s: Since she discovered that I know him, I have risen very much in her opinion.

discover.n: It gives a feeling of security and restfulness to the history recitation, that is entirely lacking in chemistry.
discover.s: If I say that William the Conqueror came over in 1492, and Columbus discovered America in 1100 or 1066 or whenever it was, that's a mere detail that the Professor overlooks.

discover.n: Old Ira Hatch has rheumatism and can't work any more; he never saved his money when he was earning good wages, so now he has to live on the town.
discover.s: The new clerk in the post office at Bonnyrigg Four Corners drank every drop of Jamaica ginger they had in stock--seven dollars' worth--before he was discovered.

discover.n: And will you promise not to think me vain?

thursday.s: Thursday evening at twilight, sitting on the doorstep.

tide.n: I can swim in salt water with the utmost ease you see my education is already being put to use!
tide.s: In the afternoon we take a walk on the cliffs, or swim, if the tide is right.

tie.s: Here is a picture of the way I look, with a bandage tied around my head in rabbit's ears.
They caught me and brought me back; and every day for a week I was tied, like a naughty puppy, to a stake in the back yard while the other children were out at recess.

tie.n: She can't imagine where he picked up his queer ideas; the family have been Church of England for generations.
tie.s: He's a Socialist--except, thank Heaven, he doesn't let his hair grow and wear red ties.

tiger.s: I'd rather find a tiger under the bed.

tight.n: Friday is sweeping day, and the maid had mixed all the papers on my desk.
tight.s: I got home to find that the box with my new blue spring dress had come, and the skirt was so tight that I couldn't sit down.

tight.n: Thank you, Daddy, a thousand times.
tight.s: You will have to wear it on cold days and keep your coat buttoned up tight.

tighten.n: 'Usually, as you know, the children are not kept after they are sixteen, but an exception was made in your case.
tighten.s: Mrs. Lippett allowed a moment of silence to fall, then resumed in a slow, placid manner extremely trying to her hearer's suddenly tightened nerves.

till.s: I promise not to write another till the middle of November.

till.n: Some of the girls sell their text-books when they're through with them, but I intend to keep mine.
till.s: Fifty-seven irregular verbs have I introduced to my brain in the past four days--I'm only hoping they'll stay till after examinations.

till.n: Then we all cut evening chapel, the state of our boots being enough of an excuse.
till.s: We didn't get back to college till half-past six--half an hour late for dinner--and we went straight in without dressing, and with perfectly unimpaired appetites!

till.n: She's getting quite interested in me, because I say such funny things.
till.s: 'Not till I came to college, and then it was only a hundred and sixty miles and we didn't eat,' said I to her.

till.n: Then I go out with Colin (the new sheep dog) and romp through the fields and get a fresh supply of ideas for the next day.
till.s: I can barely wait in the morning to dress and eat before beginning; then I write and write and write till suddenly I'm so tired that I'm limp all over.

time.n: But this particular first Wednesday, like its predecessors, finally dragged itself to a close.
time.s: It was a distressing time; and poor Jerusha Abbott, being the oldest orphan, had to bear the brunt of it.

time.n: 'It seemed to me that you showed little gratitude in holding up to ridicule the institution that has done so much for you.
time.s: Jerusha's guilty expression this time was not assumed.

time.n: We eat and sleep and study by bells.
time.s: It's very enlivening; I feel like a fire horse all of the time.

time.n: You can't imagine how different it is from the John Grier Home.
time.s: I love college and I love you for sending me--I'm very, very happy, and so excited every moment of the time that I can scarcely sleep.

time.n: I'm a foreigner in the world and I don't understand the language.
time.s: Half the time I don't know what the girls are talking about; their jokes seem to relate to a past that every one but me has shared.

time.n: I haven't told anybody though (that WOULD stamp me as queer).
time.s: I just quietly went and bought it with $1.12 of my last month's allowance; and the next time somebody mentions pickled limes, I'll know what she is talking about!

time.n: Then there is still the whole library to be read--and three empty weeks to do it in!
time.s: I'm going to have a beautiful time in vacation; there's another Freshman who lives in Texas staying behind, and we are planning to take long walks and if there's any ice--learn to skate.

time.n: Do you want to know what I bought with my money?
time.s: I. A silver watch in a leather case to wear on my wrist and get me to recitations in time.

time.n: Before I thought, I started to tell the others what an experience I was having.
time.s: Especially for me, because it was so awfully different from the asylum--I feel like an escaped convict every time I leave the campus.

time.n: I might, very usefully, put some time on Latin tonight but, there's no doubt about it, I'm a very languid Latin scholar.
time.s: Julia said she'd had a good time, but Sallie stayed to help wash the dishes.

time.n: We've finished Livy and De Senectute and are now engaged with De Amicitia (pronounced Damn Icitia).
time.s: I might, very usefully, put some time on Latin tonight but, there's no doubt about it, I'm a very languid Latin scholar.

time.n: The head nurse is very bossy.
time.s: I'm in the infirmary now, and have been here for six days; this is the first time they would let me sit up and have a pen and paper.

time.n: Here is a picture of the way I look, with a bandage tied around my head in rabbit's ears.
time.s: But I've been thinking about it all the time and I shan't get well until you forgive me.

time.n: I don't know why I am in such a reminiscent mood except that spring and the reappearance of toads always awakens the old acquisitive instinct.
time.s: Looking back dispassionately after a period of time, I will say that punishment was merited, and--if I remember rightly--adequate.

time.n: Do you mind pretending you're my uncle?
time.s: We had a beautiful time; I've longed for an uncle ever since.

time.n: He's tall and thinnish with a dark face all over lines, and the funniest underneath smile that never quite comes through but just wrinkles up the corners of his mouth.
time.s: And he has a way of making you feel right off as though you'd known him a long time.

time.n: I fell off a beam in the barn loft yesterday, while I was trying to crawl over to a nest that the black hen has stolen.
time.s: And when I came in with a scratched knee, Mrs. Semple bound it up with witch-hazel, murmuring all the time, 'Dear!

time.n: He looks like this--you can see how appropriate the name is.
time.s: I haven't had time yet to begin my immortal novel; the farm keeps me too busy.

time.n: It's a relief not having to thank Somebody for every mouthful you eat.
time.s: We have the jolliest times at the table--everybody laughs and jokes and talks at once, and we don't have to say grace beforehand.

time.n: Yours ever, Judy Abbott
time.s: Tell her about it, please, the next time you visit the J. G. H.

tintern.n: What an exquisite work it is, and how adequately it embodies his conceptions of Pantheism!
tintern.s: We are reading Wordsworth's Tintern Abbey in connection with our course in English Literature.

tiny.n: If one could derive any sort of entertainment out of the oppressive fact of a Trustee, it was something unexpected to the good.
tiny.s: She was by nature a sunny soul, and had always snatched the tiniest excuse to be amused.

tiny.n: To her surprise the matron was also, if not exactly smiling, at least appreciably affable; she wore an expression almost as pleasant as the one she donned for visitors.
tiny.s: She advanced to the office quite cheered by the tiny episode, and presented a smiling face to Mrs. Lippett.
And it's good, Daddy, I actually believe it is--just about the tiny little things that happened every day.

tiny.n: The girl that left Lock Willow at dawn was a very different person from the one who came back at night.
tiny.s: If I live to be ninety-nine I shall never forget the tiniest detail.

tip.n: Sallie whacked it with the back of my hair brush--which I shall never be able to use again--and killed the front end, but the rear fifty feet ran under the bureau and escaped.
tip.s: I tipped two cups off the tea table in trying to get away.

tiptoe.n: You'll receive it in the next mail after the other; so you won't have a very long time to think bad of me.
tiptoe.s: I'm going to tiptoe out to post this off now.

tire.n: I meant this to be just a short little thank-you note--but when I get started I seem to have a ready pen.
tire.s: Eleven pages--poor Daddy, you must be tired!

tire.n: Maybe he'll stay one week, or maybe two, or maybe three; he'll see how restful it is when he gets here.
tire.s: He's motoring through the Berkshires, and is tired and wants to rest on a nice quiet farm--if he climbs out at her doorstep some night will she have a room ready for him?

tire.n: Particularly when the day begins at dawn.
tire.s: But a girls' college is a busy place and we do get tired by the end of the day!

tire.n: There is so much noise that you can't hear the girls across the table speak unless they make their hands into a megaphone and shout.
tire.s: Dining in a room with four hundred girls is an ordeal when you are tired.

tire.n: Then I go out with Colin (the new sheep dog) and romp through the fields and get a fresh supply of ideas for the next day.
tire.s: I can barely wait in the morning to dress and eat before beginning; then I write and write and write till suddenly I'm so tired that I'm limp all over.

tissue.n: Also we had a band in blue uniforms hired from town.
tissue.s: Our class had crimson balloons--very fetching, especially as they were always getting loose and floating off--and the Freshmen wore green tissue-paper hats with long streamers.

toad.n: It went off--I was told--with quite a pop, so probably he was a fatter Trustee.
toad.s: I hope you aren't the Trustee who sat on the toad?

toad.n: And one day--well, I won't bore you with particulars--but somehow, one of the fattest, biggest, JUCIEST toads got into one of those big leather arm chairs in the Trustees' room, and that afternoon at the Trustees' meeting--But I dare say you were there and recall the rest?
toad.s: We were severely punished for our activities in this direction, but in spite of all discouragement the toads would collect.

toad.n: The only thing that keeps me from starting a collection is the fact that no rule exists against it.
toad.s: I don't know why I am in such a reminiscent mood except that spring and the reappearance of toads always awakens the old acquisitive instinct.

toast.n: Julia said she'd had a good time, but Sallie stayed to help wash the dishes.
toast.s: I had a supper party this evening consisting of Sallie and Julia and Leonora Fenton--and sardines and toasted muffins and salad and fudge and coffee.

today.s: He twirled a button Without a glance my way: But, madam, is there nothing else That we can show today?
We started to walk to town today, but mercy!

today.n: Wouldn't he make a nice villain for a detective story?
today.s: I saw a street car conductor today with one brown eye and one blue.

today.n: But if so, you've never attended a girls' college, Daddy dear.
today.s: Perhaps you think, last night being Friday, with no classes today, that I passed a nice quiet, readable evening with the set of Stevenson that I bought with my prize money?

today.n: We shall live in a perpetual breathlessness until he comes--and if he doesn't hurry, the cleaning may all have to be done over again.
today.s: He doesn't give the remotest hint as to whether he will land on the doorstep today, or two weeks from today.

today.n: I meant to have written a lot about the budding trees and the new cinder path in the athletic field, and the awful lesson we have in biology for tomorrow, and the new canoes on the lake, and Catherine Prentiss who has pneumonia, and Prexy's Angora kitten that strayed from home and has been boarding in Fergussen Hall for two weeks until a chambermaid reported it, and about my three new dresses--white and pink and blue polka dots with a hat to match--but I am too sleepy.
today.s: Dear me, Daddy, I seem to have a very ejaculatory style today; this page is peppered with exclamations.

today.n: It doesn't seem polite to ignore everybody else as though you were the only important person present.
today.s: A very beautiful lady in a very beautiful velvet dress got into the car today, and without the slightest expression sat for fifteen minutes and looked at a sign advertising suspenders.

today.n: Samuel was as excited about his clothes as any girl; he spent five times as much on dress as his wife--that appears to have been the Golden Age of husbands.
today.s: I don't believe we're so bad today as the newspapers make out.

today.n: Excuse me for being so full of Pepys; I'm writing a special topic on him.
today.s: 'Today came home my fine Camlett cloak with gold buttons, which cost me much money, and I pray God to make me able to pay for it.'

today.n: And this morning I've been out with Colin visiting all the places that you and I went to together, and remembering what you said and how you looked.
today.s: The woods today are burnished bronze and the air is full of frost.

tombstone.n: I've always hated it; but I rather like Judy.
tombstone.s: She gets the last names out of the telephone book--you'll find Abbott on the first page--and she picks the Christian names up anywhere; she got Jerusha from a tombstone.

tombstone.n: We were kept in chapel twenty minutes later than usual to listen to a speech about womanly women.
tombstone.s: We had tombstone for dessert (milk and gelatin flavoured with vanilla).
But just as I was turning to the end to read the inscription on my tombstone, I woke up.

tommy.n: Mrs. Lippett in the office, And I think she's mad.
tommy.s: she cut into Tommy's chant with a note of sharp anxiety.

tommy.n: Even the most hardened little orphan felt sympathy for an erring sister who was summoned to the office to face an annoyed matron; and Tommy liked Jerusha even if she did sometimes jerk him by the arm and nearly scrub his nose off.
tommy.s: Tommy piously intoned, but his accent was not entirely malicious.

tommy.n: There are lots of troubles in the world!
tommy.s: Sentimental Tommy (the tortoise-shell cat) has disappeared; we are afraid he has been caught in a trap.

tomorrow.n: It grieves me to tell you that Buttercup (the spotted cow with one horn, Mother of Lesbia) has done a disgraceful thing.
tomorrow.s: We're going to begin threshing oats tomorrow; a steam engine is coming and three extra men.

tomorrow.n: You might think, from this account of our activities, that the house was not already immaculate; but I assure you it was!
tomorrow.s: Mrs. Dowd is engaged to come tomorrow to wash the windows (in the exigency of the moment, we waive our suspicions in regard to the piglet).

tomorrow.n: We want it to come very gradually in the distant future, when we shall all be prepared and able to sustain the shock.
tomorrow.s: We don't want the social revolution to come tomorrow morning; it would be too upsetting.
Very busy time--commencement in ten days, examinations tomorrow; lots of studying, lots of packing, and the outdoor world so lovely that it hurts you to stay inside.

tomorrow.n: The professor doesn't seem to realize that we are taking any other subject.
tomorrow.s: We are getting the most dreadful lessons in philosophy--all of Schopenhauer for tomorrow.

tomorrow.n: How relieved they'll be when five o'clock comes and you pat them on the head and take yourselves off!
tomorrow.s: Tomorrow is the first Wednesday in the month--a weary day for the John Grier Home.

tomorrow.n: It's the sweetest book you ever saw--it is, truly.
tomorrow.s: I've finished the second draft of my book and am going to begin the third tomorrow morning at half-past seven.

tone.n: You must remember that you are writing to a Trustee of the John Grier Home.'
tone.s: I hope that they will always be respectful in tone and will reflect credit on your training.

tonight.n: The Freshman's lot is not a happy one.
tonight.s: I meant to write you a nice, cheerful, entertaining letter tonight, but I'm too sleepy--and scared.
This is an extra letter in the middle of the month because I'm rather lonely tonight.

tonight.n: We've finished Livy and De Senectute and are now engaged with De Amicitia (pronounced Damn Icitia).
tonight.s: I might, very usefully, put some time on Latin tonight but, there's no doubt about it, I'm a very languid Latin scholar.

tonight.n: I can't think of anything I'd rather have; it's such a respectable relationship.
tonight.s: Sallie has one and Julia and Leonora each two, and they were all comparing them tonight.
Tonight I have a pressing engagement with the Ablative Absolute.
We shall have to swim to chapel tonight.

tonight.n: As soon as dinner is over, Sallie and Julia and Marty Keene and Eleanor Pratt (friends of mine, but you don't know them) and I are going to put on short skirts and walk 'cross country to Crystal Spring Farm and have a fried chicken and waffle supper, and then have Mr. Crystal Spring drive us home in his buckboard.
tonight.s: We are supposed to be inside the campus at seven, but we are going to stretch a point tonight and make it eight.

tonsil.n: 'What were your parents thinking of not to have those tonsils out when you were a baby?'
tonsil.s: For two days I've been laid up with swollen tonsils; I can just swallow hot milk, and that is all.
'What were your parents thinking of not to have those tonsils out when you were a baby?'

tonsillitis.n: I'm in the infirmary now, and have been here for six days; this is the first time they would let me sit up and have a pen and paper.
tonsillitis.s: I didn't know it, but I was just sickening for tonsillitis and grippe and lots of things mixed.

tooth.n: No, it isn't either; I've just looked in the book.
tooth.s: I'm not sure myself, but I think it's a missing link, like a bird with teeth or a lizard with wings.

toothache.n: 'Whatever sky's above me, I've a heart for any fate.'
toothache.s: I'm going to regard them (even toothaches) as interesting experiences, and be glad to know what they feel like.

topic.n: I've just been reading this letter over and it sounds pretty un-cheerful.
topic.s: But can't you guess that I have a special topic due Monday morning and a review in geometry and a very sneezy cold?

topic.n: V. Lou McMahon slipped and fell this afternoon at basket ball, and she: A. Dislocated her shoulder.
topic.s: I am writing a special topic upon the Sources of Shakespeare's Plays.
Excuse me for being so full of Pepys; I'm writing a special topic on him.

topmost.n: I meant to write you a nice, cheerful, entertaining letter tonight, but I'm too sleepy--and scared.
topmost.s: On the topmost branches of her family tree there's a superior breed of monkeys with very fine silky hair and extra long tails.

torchlight.n: I am beginning chemistry, a most unusual study.
torchlight.s: Election comes next Saturday, and we're going to have a torchlight procession in the evening, no matter who wins.

torchlight.n: We're very important persons now in '258.'
torchlight.s: Sallie was elected, and we had a torchlight parade with transparencies saying, 'McBride for Ever,' and a band consisting of fourteen pieces (three mouth organs and eleven combs).

tortoise.n: There are lots of troubles in the world!
tortoise.s: Sentimental Tommy (the tortoise-shell cat) has disappeared; we are afraid he has been caught in a trap.

toss.n: I promise not to write another till the middle of November.
toss.s: If my letters bore you, you can always toss them into the wastebasket.

tot.n: Jerusha assembled her charges, straightened their rumpled frocks, wiped their noses, and started them in an orderly and willing line towards the dining-room to engage themselves for a blessed half hour with bread and milk and prune pudding.
tot.s: Her special care was room F, where eleven little tots, from four to seven, occupied eleven little cots set in a row.

touch.n: In imagination she followed first one equipage, then another, to the big houses dotted along the hillside.
touch.s: Jerusha leaned forward watching with curiosity--and a touch of wistfulness--the stream of carriages and automobiles that rolled out of the asylum gates.

touch.n: An automobile flashed past the window; Mrs. Lippett glanced after it.
touch.s: Jerusha dropped into the nearest chair and waited with a touch of breathlessness.

touch.n: On looking it up, I find that she was right.
touch.s: In trigonometry the Professor and I had a disagreement touching a little matter of logarithms.

touch.n: And a big square mahogany table--I'm going to spend the summer with my elbows spread out on it, writing a novel.
touch.s: It's big and square and empty, with adorable old-fashioned furniture and windows that have to be propped up on sticks and green shades trimmed with gold that fall down if you touch them.

touch.n: Whereas a woman--whether she is interested in babies or microbes or husbands or poetry or servants or parallelograms or gardens or Plato or bridge--is fundamentally and always interested in clothes.
touch.s: It's the one touch of nature that makes the whole world kin.

touch.n: Julia has a trunkful of the most ravishing new clothes--an evening gown of rainbow Liberty crepe that would be fitting raiment for the angels in Paradise.
touch.s: You men ought to leave intrigue to women; you haven't a light enough touch.

touch.n: Such a little half hour we had together!
touch.s: I want to have you close by where I can touch you and make sure you are tangible.

tough.n: While the others are hopping about in the air, I can dodge under their feet and grab the ball.
tough.s: I'm little of course, but terribly quick and wiry and tough.

tout.n: There would be danger of quelque chose affreuse happening, parceque j'ai perdue ma humilite d'autre fois et j'ai peur that I would just break out quelque jour et smash every cup and saucer dans la maison.
tout.s: Je suis tres heureuse about the farm, parceque je n'ai jamais been on a farm dans ma vie and I'd hate to retourner chez John Grier, et wash dishes tout l'ete.
Je ne peux pas send des mes nouvelles parceque je suis dans French class et j'ai peur que Monsieur le Professeur is going to call on me tout de suite.

tower.n: There are three other girls on the same floor of the tower--a Senior who wears spectacles and is always asking us please to be a little more quiet, and two Freshmen named Sallie McBride and Julia Rutledge Pendleton.
tower.s: My room is up in a tower that used to be the contagious ward before they built the new infirmary.

tower.n: It's late afternoon--the sun is just setting (a cold yellow colour) behind some colder violet hills, and I am up in my window seat using the last light to write to you.
tower.s: All the world that I see from my tower is draped in white and the flakes are coming down as big as pop-corns.

tower.n: Eleven pages--poor Daddy, you must be tired!
tower.s: My tower is just a trifle lonely; when nine people occupy a house that was built for four hundred, they do rattle around a bit.

tower.n: My English instructor stopped me on the way out from chapel last night, and said it was a charming piece of work except for the sixth line, which had too many feet.
tower.s: A poem entitled, 'From my Tower', appears in the February Monthly--on the first page, which is a very great honour for a Freshman.

tower.n: I believe I am sleepy after all.
tower.s: That's the clock in the chapel tower striking twelve.
A prince in a tower with a velvet tunic and yellow curls.

town.n: Broiled lobster (35 cents), and for dessert, buckwheat cakes and maple syrup (15 cents).
town.s: Once we walked into town--four miles--and stopped at a restaurant where the college girls go for dinner.

town.n: I am going to make you a present of it on your eighty-third birthday.
town.s: So, if you really don't object--When I went into town yesterday, I saw the sweetest cap of Cluny lace trimmed with lavender ribbon.

town.n: He's her father's youngest brother, but she doesn't know him very intimately.
town.s: Being in town on business, he decided to run out to the college and call on his niece.
We started to walk to town today, but mercy!

town.n: Also about a dozen funny people, like clowns in a circus, to keep the spectators entertained between events.
town.s: Also we had a band in blue uniforms hired from town.

town.n: There's to be an ice-cream social at the schoolhouse next Saturday evening.
town.s: Old Ira Hatch has rheumatism and can't work any more; he never saved his money when he was earning good wages, so now he has to live on the town.

town.n: Yesterday he brought me some shoe-strings and a jar of cold cream (I sunburned all the skin off my nose before I got my new hat) and a blue Windsor tie and a bottle of blacking all for ten cents.
town.s: Our postman not only delivers letters, but he runs errands for us in town, at five cents an errand.

toy.n: Or else, dear friend, a long farewell.
toy.s: Come, leave your sports and earthly toys And join me in celestial joys.

track.n: He will never answer your letters, nor in the slightest particular take any notice of them.
track.s: Since you have no family with whom to correspond, he desires you to write in this way; also, he wishes to keep track of your progress.

tragedy.n: It's the kind of character that I am going to develop.
tragedy.s: Anybody can rise to a crisis and face a crushing tragedy with courage, but to meet the petty hazards of the day with a laugh--I really think that requires SPIRIT.

trail.n: Jervis Pendleton if this book should ever roam, Box its ears and send it home.
trail.s: It's entitled, On the Trail, and sprawled across the front page in a funny little-boy hand:

trail.n: It looks well read--the marks of his grimy little hands are frequent!
trail.s: He spent the summer here once after he had been ill, when he was about eleven years old; and he left On the Trail behind.

trail.n: of course half of us went in ankle deep.
trail.s: The trail led over a hill, through a cornfield, and into a swamp where we had to leap lightly from hummock to hummock.

trail.n: Then up a hill through some woods and in at a barn window!
trail.s: We kept losing the trail, and we wasted twenty-five minutes over that swamp.

trail.n: The fox thought he had us there, but we fooled him.
trail.s: But we didn't go through; we circumnavigated the barn and picked up the trail where it issued by way of a low shed roof on to the top of a fence.

trail.n: I thought we were paying a social call, and started to shake hands, but it seems we were only buying hats--at least Julia was.
trail.s: A perfectly beautiful lady with yellow hair and a long black silk trailing gown came to meet us with a welcoming smile.
Then we came down by moonlight, and, when we reached the wood trail where it was dark, by the light of an electric bulb that he had in his pocket.

train.n: You must remember that you are writing to a Trustee of the John Grier Home.'
train.s: I hope that they will always be respectful in tone and will reflect credit on your training.

train.n: It's a funny sensation, isn't it?
train.s: I travelled yesterday for four hours in a train.
Observe with what precision I obey rules--due to my training in the John Grier Home.

train.n: The aim of the John Grier Home (as you doubtless know and heartily approve of) is to turn the ninety-seven orphans into ninety-seven twins.
train.s: It doesn't seem possible, does it, considering the eighteen years of training that I've had?

train.n: She was furious with me for taking him off; it seems he's an unusually rich and desirable uncle.
train.s: But he had to run for his train the minute he got back and he barely saw Julia at all.

train.n: We had an awful lot of trouble getting permission.
train.s: Our innocent prattle appeared to amuse him and he waited for a later train in order to take tea in the study.

train.n: I never heard of meals being served in trains before, and I inadvertently said so.
train.s: We came back Saturday night and had our dinner in the train, at little tables with pink lamps and negro waiters.

train.n: 'Where on earth were you brought up?'
train.s: I never heard of meals being served in trains before, and I inadvertently said so.

train.n: Besides--she wished to show him off in church.
train.s: She is awfully troubled to think that she didn't train him better when he was small and helpless and she had the chance.

train.n: All the furniture was carved and upholstered and gorgeous; the people I met were beautifully dressed and low-voiced and well-bred, but it's the truth, Daddy, I never heard one word of real talk from the time we arrived until we left.
train.s: The material atmosphere of that house was crushing; I didn't draw a deep breath until I was on an express train coming back.

train.n: And where do you think we were going?
train.s: Master Jervie dropped in again last Friday--but he chose a most unpropitious time, for Sallie and Julia and I were just running to catch a train.
All the way in the train the rails kept singing, 'You're going to see Daddy-Long-Legs.'

train.n: And you were pretty dazed, too.
train.s: I was so dazed when I got to the station that I almost took a train for St Louis.

tramp.n: Our clothes were drenched before we reached home but our spirits not even damp.
tramp.s: We went for a long tramp this morning and got caught in a storm.

tramp.n: We climbed to the top of 'Sky Hill' this morning where Master Jervie and I once cooked supper--it doesn't seem possible that it was nearly two years ago.
tramp.s: We are tramping over the hills and reading and writing, and having a nice, restful time.

tramp.n: And she doesn't curl her hair any longer.
tramp.s: She used to laugh when he tramped in mud or dropped ashes on the floor, but now--you should hear her scold!

transmit.n: As you observe, I am here, and have been for five days.
